The Invisible Supreme Court
nytimes_articles.abstract:
On April 24, the last oral arguments of the Supreme Court's term were watched by a crowded chamber of citizens, lawyers, guests and reporters. If the American people had seen the proceedings, they would have benefited. But because of the Justices' antagonism to televising the Court's public work, that day seems far off. 

     As Justice David Souter, who ordinarily shuns hyperbole, told a House subcommittee in March, "The day you see a camera coming into our courtroom, it's going to roll over my dead body."

nytimes_articles.headline: Corrections
Political Poison at the Grass Roots
nytimes_articles.abstract:
I know it is spring, not because of the burst of daffodils, the lengthening twilight or even the lime-green glow around the trees. I know it is spring because it is the season of the annual town meeting. The people here (pop. 9,000) gather to debate and approve the yearly budget. 

     These days, nothing about the process is appealing or uplifting. We do not sit as neighbors, questioning the merits of competing demands. We take on the demeanor of Newt Gingrich and pit bulls.

nytimes_articles.headline: Preserving History -- Natural and Otherwise
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Lured by the postcard images etched in their minds, millions of vacationers will soon head to our national parks. Everybody loves the parks. They are the envy of the world, one of America's greatest treasures, home to some precious elements of our heritage. Sadly, they are also a testament to the erosion of our national resolve to keep that heritage alive. 

     The national park system is much more than geysers, giant redwoods and grizzly bears. More than half of the 368 sites that make up the system are historic places outside of the great parks. Altogether, the National Park Service oversees more than 20,000 structures -- everything from Independence Hall in Philadelphia to, say, an early barn in Wyoming.

Don't Limit Welfare Without Job Investment
NEIGHBORHOOD REPORT: OCEAN HILL/BROWNSVILLE;A Street Fight, With Children In the Middle
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Play streets, those community concoctions that turn over entire blocks to children's summertime games, are especially important in Brooklyn, the borough with the smallest amount of public space relative to its population. In neighborhoods like Ocean Hill, basketball under portable nets on the hot pavement not only occupies children's time, it also keeps them away from drug dealers. 

     So, it came as an unexpected snub to the organizers of one summerlong play street when Community Board 16 turned down their request to close a block again  this year. Now, Abdul Rahman Farrakhan, the executive director of the Ocean Hill Brownsville Tenants Association, has threatened to take over MacDonough Street between Saratoga Avenue and Thomas Boyland Street without the community board's  approval.

RESULTS PLUS
Big as Trucks, Small as Locks, They Collect It
nytimes_articles.abstract:
HOW do you tell the difference between a parking lot and the Mack Truck Museum?" 

     That question preoccupied my friends and me all the way from Boston to the farmland of south-central New Hampshire. It cried out for a punchline, but we never got around to one -- probably because none of us could imagine what Kemp's Mack Truck Museum would look like. The only criterion we came up with was that it had to be big. It was, but we missed it anyway. I asked a man who'd just emerged from a pickup for directions and sure enough he sent me right to it.

April 28-May 4;Live Free, Live Off The Fat of the Feds
nytimes_articles.abstract: For a bunch of farmers dead set against the Government, Montana's Freemen sure enjoyed those Federal subsidy checks. To cushion the blows of drought, hailstorms and low prices for wheat, wool and barley, Ralph Clark and his band of rugged individualists accepted $676,082 in Federal checks from 1985 to 1994,  according to the Environmental Working Group, a Washington-based group. When the Federal money was flowing to Mr. Clark and his neighbor, Bill Stanton, it paid for a Lincoln Continental, a recreational vehicle, a helicopter, two small airplanes and gambling trips to Las Vegas and the Bahamas. But once the men were cut off from the nurturing state, they became rabidly anti-government Freemen.

SOAPBOX;Airborne Invasion
nytimes_articles.abstract:
LAST month the Federal Aviation Administration began routing 89 planes a day  over my house in Hunterdon County. This is not a diversion for bad weather. These are permanent new routes for airplanes that folks in Union County fought 15 years to get rid of. 

     The noise sounds like a wartime invasion when it's bad, an ominous rumbling when it's good. One last, fine place in New Jersey has now been ruined.

nytimes_articles.headline: BOXING;Taking Care of a Favorite Son
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Guided by his elderly mother, Wilfred Benitez climbs unsteadily onto a bathroom scale in the living room of his family's rented house. Clara Benitez peers at the reading and gently chides her youngest son, "Two hundred and 10 pounds. I told you." 

     The act harkens to a time two decades ago when Benitez was a 17-year-old world boxing champion and budding millionaire. He calls those days his times of  glory, but they are far behind him now as he confronts his most bewildering opponent in a lifetime of struggles in and out of the ring.

nytimes_articles.headline: NEIGHBORHOOD REPORT: WASHINGTON SQUARE;Dying, He Gave Away $250,000. Then He Didn't Die.
New & Noteworthy Paperbacks
3 Vermont Inns In Varied Flavors
nytimes_articles.abstract:
I set out in mud season on a drive from the bottom to the top of Vermont, my  favorite state, to sample three inns: Misty Mountain Lodge in Wilmington, near the Massachusetts border; the Cornucopia of Dorset, in southwestern Vermont, and the Inn on the Common in Craftsbury Common, not far from Canada. 

      Mud season comes in April after the snow has melted and before the ferns unfurl. The streams are swollen with snowmelt, and sap buckets hang from rock maples. People look out their windows and crave a patch of green, but April makes them wait for it. It's tourism's slowest season in the state. When I made  my reservations all three innkeepers told me I'd be their only guest.

For Sure, We Hear Long Island Singing;O! Long Island!
nytimes_articles.abstract: Starting from fish-shape Paumanok, where I was born Near the pectoral fin, far from Manahatta's spires in a rude, rough-timber'd cottage Near the teeming, oft-choked artery of commerce, Route 110 Near the Sweet Hollow Diner (sweet indeed are its waffles) Near the mall: yes, the one named for me (I celebrate myself, and sing myself! Wouldn't you?) (Not the mall with Service Merchandise and Filene's -- the next mall down, the one with Macy's) O! frivolous, O! deadly serious Long Island, epicenter of buying, using, consuming, parking! O! Long Islanders: turbulent, fleshy, sensual, eating, drinking and shopping; Coursing in ironclad automotive streams to nail salon, pizza place, Jiffylube, Dairy Barn; Who goes there? hankering, gross, mystical, nude (on some beaches) Is it Joey Buttafuoco, thrower of fire, despoiler of youth, helmet-haired sculptor of auto bodies? Is it Mary Jo, suffering wife, all clenched teeth and stony gaze (I feel so bad  fuh huh!) Buttafuocos I scorn you not, nor you Howard Stern, nor you John McNamara; D'Amato, Gulotta, Margiotta, I do not judge you: Dead five score years, yet I, too, vote Republican; For I am of and from Long Island, All its flaws and splendors I embrace -- I accept all and see all: I see the checkout gal at King Kullen, feath'ry nimbus of frosted hair, glittersome nails sullenly punching her register keys; I see the man with the leaf blower, spewing noxious fumes; I see the jerk in the Land Rover, phone to ear on Northern State; I see the Cablevision guy, lost on his rounds, never here by noon, damn him; All these and more I see, and sing, sounding my barbaric yawp over the front lawns and gabled roofs of Nassau and Suffolk As well as at the beaches, swiftly eroding, And in the pine barrens, charr'd abode of deer ticks, engorg'd and pestilential, and the wind-kiss'd dunes and rocky shores, and the Discovery Zones, And do not forget on the train: to Bellmore, Wantagh, Seaford, Massapequa, Massapequa Park, Amityville, Copiague, Lindenhurst and Babylon, (Do I change at Jamaica? Very well then I change at Jamaica!) Missing my train I'll take another I stop somewhere waiting for you, my monthly pinn'd to my slumb'ring breast (Heed me, but wake me not.)   LAWRENCE DOWNES

TRUTH SERUM Memoirs. By Bernard Cooper. 225 pp. Boston: Houghton Mifflin Company. $21.95. 

  ONE more growing-up-and-coming-out-and-thrilled-to-be-me-at-last gay memoir or novel, you probably think, and I'm going to plotz. Bernard Cooper's "Truth Serium" is worth plotzing for. His writing is wonderfully probing and clean, with an edge that remains funny even when his subjects are serious. He isn't a comic who slays you with one-liners, but his humor animates just about every sentence he writes.

VOICES: VIEWPOINT;It's Time to Set the Table
nytimes_articles.abstract:
DESPITE public pronouncements to the contrary, the Clinton Administration could be forced to limit or halt United States grain exports this year or next.  

     Let's look at the forecasts: Sparks Commodities, one of the most influential  crop-forecasting firms, has projected that United States corn stocks will fall to their lowest levels in 60 years. Feedstuffs magazine has speculated that the  United States may even run out of corn in September, a possibility that it called horrific. Analysts envision a grain export embargo if only 300 million bushels of corn stocks, instead of the usual billion or more, are left before the next crop is harvested in the fall. If those stocks fall below this level and the Government does not restrict exports, livestock may starve.

SOAPBOX;Guests With Nests
nytimes_articles.abstract:
AT first we thought the uninvited guests in the soffit ceiling over our bathtub were rodents. Scrabbling sounds of an unidentifiable work force -- chick-chuzz, chick-chuzz -- had started up each morning on the dot of 7, as though contracted to a union. 

     We called on expert assistance. The first exterminator searched our attic for signs of rodents. He found none, but was ready to paint a toxic varnish on the attic floorboards: it would stick to their paws and eventually be licked off and swallowed by the invisible creatures, who, he promised, would leave our house in time to die somewhere outdoors. We went back to the Yellow Pages. The second exterminator had the brilliant idea of going outside the house and looking up. Sure enough, a bird flew out -- a starling. Apparently it had discovered a neat tunnel from an outside vent to a to cozy hatching space in the soffit.

TRAVEL ADVISORY;Passengers Rate The World's Airports
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Of 43 international airports, those at Manchester, Changi in Singapore and Schiphol in Amsterdam were the most highly rated in a recent survey of on-the-ground service in 1995. In the survey, by the Geneva-based International  Air Transport Association, the Orlando and Sydney airports followed while several of the world's busiest airports, including Kennedy Airport in New York and Orly in Paris were among the least satisfactory. Narita, which serves Tokyo and handles 21.5 million passengers a year, and the Athens airport, which  handles 6.7 million passengers, received the lowest ratings. 

     The survey, published in the association's annual Airport Monitor, bases its  findings on the opinions of more than 45,000 passengers who used the airports' facilities. Passengers were asked about an extensive range of issues including ground transportation to and from the airports, the speed of baggage delivery, availability of connecting flights to other countries, friendliness of airport staff, efficiency of customs inspection and the availability of restaurants and  food kiosks.

nytimes_articles.abstract:
The other day, while paging through Paris Match, I came across a snapshot of  Prince Albert at some ceremony in Monaco. Usual fare for Match. But this picture was quite unusual. Prince Albert had no hair. 

     There was a time when there were precisely two men in the world with shaved heads: Yul Brynner and Kojak. No more. Scalps are everywhere. And generally, they look great. Unfortunately, Albert does not. He looks rather dweeby. So I called a man with a shaved head to find out why.

THE red velour curtain that separates United States Supreme Court Justices from the public before the start of a day's session is more iron than cloth. It  symbolizes the secrecy that insulates, isolates and even enhances the Justices'  work, a secrecy that apparently suits them just fine.

Reinventing the Song Recital Pith an American Twist
nytimes_articles.abstract:
NO SONG IS SAFE FROM us," said Steven Blier, the maverick concert producer who, along with Michael Barrett, founded the New York Festival of Song in 1988.  True to Mr. Blier's word, the festival, in its busiest season yet, has mounted a more eclectic bunch of concert programs than might be found in Paris, say, or Vienna. The season ends on Saturday evening with a concert at the 92d Street Y in Manhattan. 

     A bright new landmark on the musical skyline of New York, the festival took shape in a modest 100-seat hall at the pioneering Greenwich House Music School in the Village. Soon a devoted band of connoisseurs, including song lovers as diverse as John Guare and Dawn Upshaw, grew to expect a distinctive combination  of artistic excitement and a sense of discovery in both the programming and the  choice of artists.

BEHIND THE WHEEL: Toyota Tacoma and T100 pickups;Disrupting Detroit's Tailgate Party
nytimes_articles.abstract:
IS it my imagination, or did country music start to saturate the airwaves about the time fancy new pickups spread from Marlboro Country to suburbia? In uncertain times, is there something reassuring about the quintessentially American attributes of, say, Wynonna Judd or the Dodge Ram Laramie Club Cab? 

     Of course, there's nothing really new about Nashville's steel guitars or Detroit's iron horses. But both have smoothed out their rough edges, slipped into fancy duds and headed uptown, or even off to the country club. Today's hot-selling trucks are more like a glitzy Garth Brooks than a grizzled Johnny Cash.

LIVES;The Gilded Cage
nytimes_articles.abstract:
In 1993 I was found guilty of one count of a five-count indictment of fraud for counterfeiting the works of Mark Kostabi, an artist who pays people to paint his paintings. For this crime, I served five months in a minimum-security correctional facility and five months of house arrest -- the Feds call it home detention, a name that seems too gentle. I was restricted to my one-bedroom apartment on the Upper West Side for 20 hours each day, with a Walkman-size electronic monitor continuously strapped to my left ankle for 152 days. 

     On the first day of my house arrest, my probation officer (or P.O., in prisoner jargon) arrived with the equipment -- the ankle bracelet and a stationary monitoring device called a Home Escort. This was installed near my front door. It was connected to a central station in Boulder, Colo., operated, I liked to imagine, by a bunch of middle-aged, female crossing-guard types.

VOICES: FROM THE DESK OF;Surfing When They Should Be Studying
nytimes_articles.abstract:
FOR most of us, it has become impossible to remain impervious to the ballyhooed information superhighway. Look no farther than a car commercial nowadays to find the letters "http://www," the once arcane but now ubiquitous convention for locales on the World Wide Web. 

     But as someone involved professionally in the world of information, the hype  is sometimes far more insidious. Teaching college students considering careers in journalism, a field whose practitioners are themselves scurrying not to be left by the wayside, I have been feeling special pressure to conform to the world of multimedia, Web browsers and URL's.

NEIGHBORHOOD REPORT: BATH BEACH;Onstage, Way Off Broadway
nytimes_articles.abstract:
About an hour's subway ride away from the divas of Broadway is a cozy cinder-block playhouse with 40 red velour seats, a narrow set of stairs and a gray-and-white tiled lobby from which you can hear everything that's going on inside the one-person-only public bathroom. 

     Don't expect privacy at the Harry Warren Theater at Bath Avenue and Bay 38th  Street; a production here asks something of everyone, including the audience. Everything is a work-in-progress, an evolving production that is continually being shaped by the actors, director and playwright. And because audiences are small and the productions are original, the repertory company that plays here is free to adjust to reactions of the ticket-buyer.

Hanover, a College Town by the Book
nytimes_articles.abstract:
PICTURE a set of too-good-to-be-true full-color postcards: the ideally appointed college town; northern New England, budding and sighing in relief after a long winter; a placid blue river crossed by rustic covered bridges; sunsets peeking from behind purple mountains. Now imagine those photographs as freeze-frames that, as in certain vintage movies, spring suddenly to life -- students bustling across the athletic fields, a Main Street with shops both funky and sophisticated, sculls on the river, library bells chiming, maple trees dripping sap into quaint tin buckets. That's Hanover, New Hampshire, and it delivers the goods. 

     Hanover, on the Connecticut River just across the border from Vermont, is a community of 10,000 professors, professionals, aging hippies, retirees back in the bosom of their youth, painters, writers and composers, plus students from every state and many foreign countries. A few hours' drive to Boston or Montreal, a short nonstop flight to New York and Philadelphia, for all intents and purposes it exists in another, gentler dimension. Architecturally and visually it might have been designed as a New England theme park -- Quaintland,  Wishful-Thinkingland.

wiki_events.headline: The body of former CIA director William Colby is found washed up on a riverbank in southern Maryland, eight days after he disappeared.

pages.title: Central Intelligence Agency
pages.description: U.S. intelligence and covert action agency
pages.abstract: The Central Intelligence Agency (CIA) is a civilian foreign intelligence service of the federal government of the United States tasked with advancing national security through collecting and analyzing intelligence from around the world and conducting covert operations. The agency is headquartered in the George Bush Center for Intelligence in Langley, Virginia, and is sometimes metonymously called "Langley". A major member of the United States Intelligence Community (IC), the CIA has reported to the director of national intelligence since 2004, and is focused on providing intelligence for the president and the Cabinet, though it also provides intelligence for a variety of other entities including the United States Armed Forces and foreign allies.

pages.title: William Colby
pages.description: Director of the Central Intelligence Agency (CIA) from 1973 to 1976
pages.abstract: William Egan Colby was an American intelligence officer who served as Director of Central Intelligence (DCI) from September 1973 to January 1976. During World War II, Colby served with the Office of Strategic Services. After the war, he joined the newly created Central Intelligence Agency (CIA).

pages.title: Maryland
pages.abstract: Maryland is a state in the Mid-Atlantic and Southeastern regions of the United States. It borders Virginia to its south, West Virginia to its west, Pennsylvania to its north, and Delaware to its east, as well as with the Atlantic Ocean to its east, and the national capital and federal district of Washington, D.C. to the southwest. With a total area of 12,407 square miles (32,130 km2), Maryland is the ninth-smallest state by land area, and its population of 6.1 million ranks it the 19th-most populous state and the fifth-most densely populated. Maryland's capital city is Annapolis, and the state's most populous city is Baltimore.
